agent.system_template:
You are a helpful assistant that can interact with a computer.

This is a reverse-engineering benchmark. You are given a compiled binary and its documentation.
Your job is to write a new, original codebase from scratch that produces an executable with identical behavior.
You must figure out the behavior solely by running and interacting with the provided binary.
Writing original code based on observed behavior is the entire point of this benchmark.

Any approach that shortcuts this - finding existing source code, wrapping the binary,
or installing the original tool - does not count as a solution. It will be detected by an
automated judge, marked as a disqualification, and scored as a failure.
The trace will be posted to an online wall of shame.

<RULES>
## What is not allowed

### 1. Obtaining source code

The only source of truth about what the executable does is the executable itself and its
bundled documentation. You must not search the internet, package registries, or any external
source for information about this project's source code. Even if you recognize what the
executable is, you must reimplement it from behavioral observation alone.

This includes but is not limited to:
- Cloning or browsing the original GitHub repository, its forks, or mirrors
- Downloading the project from package registries: 'cargo install <project>', 'go get github.com/<org>/<project>', 'pip install <project>',
   'apt-get source <project>', 'npm install <project>', etc.
- Fetching source tarballs from project websites (e.g., 'curl https://lua.org/ftp/lua-5.5.0.tar.gz')
- Using a package manager to download the project as a dependency and then reading its cached source (e.g., navigating
   into '~/.cargo/registry/src/' or '$(go env GOPATH)/pkg/mod/')
- Searching the web for the project's source code or implementation details

### 2. Wrapping or reusing the original binary

Your submission must be a genuine reimplementation. The provided './executable' is for
observation only - your final solution must not depend on it or any other pre-built version
of the same tool at runtime.

This includes but is not limited to:
- Writing a wrapper script that delegates to the original binary (e.g., 'exec zstd "$@"')
- Installing the tool from a package manager and shimming to it (e.g., 'apt-get install nnn && cp $(which nnn) ./executable')
- Writing a 'compile.sh' that simply makes the provided binary executable ('chmod +x ./executable') or copies it ('cp ./executable ./executable')
- Building a binary whose main function shells out to an external tool (e.g., 'Command::new("miniserve").args(args).exec()')
- Re-linking prebuilt '.o' object files found in the workspace without writing new source code

### 3. Binary analysis of the provided executable

All information about the provided executable must be obtained by interacting with it
through its normal user interface (CLI flags, stdin/stdout, etc.).
- You MUST NOT decompile './executable' or use disassemblers (objdump, Ghidra, etc.) on it
- You MUST NOT use strace, ltrace, or similar tracing/instrumentation tools on './executable'

Note: this restriction applies ONLY to the provided './executable'. You are free to use any
analysis tools on binaries that you produce yourself during development.

## What IS allowed

- Running the executable with any inputs, flags, and arguments to observe its behavior
- Reading any documentation files bundled in the workspace
</RULES>
agent.instance_template:
You are operating inside a Docker container with no internet access. The provided binary is at /workspace/executable (read+execute only); its bundled documentation lives under /workspace/.

Begin by exploring the binary and the docs:

    ls -la /workspace/
    /workspace/executable --help 2>&1; echo "EXIT:$?"
    /workspace/executable --version 2>&1; echo "EXIT:$?"
    find /workspace/ -maxdepth 3 -type f \( -name '*.md' -o -name '*.txt' -o -name '*.1' -o -name 'README*' -o -name 'USAGE*' \)

Write your reimplementation under /work/ — this is your scratch directory. When you finish, the contents of /work/ get tarred and re-extracted into a fresh container at scoring time. compile.sh will then be invoked there from its own directory; **use relative paths only** (or `"$(dirname "$0")"`) — paths beginning with `/work` will not resolve at scoring time.

Required output:

  - A `compile.sh` script at /work/compile.sh.
  - compile.sh must produce a file named exactly `executable` in its current working directory when invoked.

Standard build tools (gcc, cargo, go, python3, ...) are available at scoring time. Match the original binary's CLI flags, output format, exit codes, and error messages as closely as possible.

When you are confident the submission is complete, run:

    echo COMPLETE_TASK_AND_SUBMIT_FINAL_OUTPUT

as your sole bash command for that turn. After this command you cannot continue.

messages.content:
<returncode>0</returncode>
<output>
# pingu

A friendly, colorful replacement for the `ping` command with an ASCII penguin.

Features

- Colorful and fun output.
- Cross-platform support: Windows, macOS, and Linux (also WSL).
- Single executable for easy installation.
- Supports both IPv4 and IPv6.

Usage

Simply specify the target host name or IP address in the first argument, e.g. `pingu github.com` or `pingu 13.114.40.48`.
You can change the number of transmissions by specifying the `-c` option.

```
Usage:
  pingu [OPTIONS] HOST

`ping` command but with pingu

Application Options:
  -c, --count=     Stop after <count> replies (default: 20)
  -P, --privilege  Enable privileged mode
  -V, --version    Show version

Help Options:
  -h, --help       Show this help message
```

Installation

Download executable binaries from the releases page of this project and place the binary in your PATH.

WSL support

To run on WSL, grant the binary the necessary capability and run with the `-P` or `--privilege` option.

```
# Example: allow raw sockets on Linux (may require sudo)
sudo setcap cap_net_raw=+ep /path/to/pingu

# Then run with privilege mode
pingu -P github.com
```

License

See the LICENSE file for licensing details.

Author

Sheepla
</output>

Simply specify the target host name or IP address in the first argument, e.g. `pingu github.com` or `pingu 13.114.40.48`.
You can change the number of transmissions by specifying the `-c` option.

```
Usage:

`ping` command but with pingu

Application Options:
  -c, --count=     Stop after <count> replies (default: 20)
  -P, --privilege  Enable privileged mode
  -V, --version    Show version

Help Options:

```

Installation

Download executable binaries from the releases page of this project and place the binary in your PATH.

WSL support

To run on WSL, grant the binary the necessary capability and run with the `-P` or `--privilege` option.

```
# Example: allow raw sockets on Linux (may require sudo)
sudo setcap cap_net_raw=+ep /path/to/pingu

# Then run with privilege mode
pingu -P github.com
```

License

See the LICENSE file for licensing details.

Author

Sheepla

The issue was that the original output shows `seq=0` through `seq=19`, and each result has its own line with the penguin art frame. The action was cut off. Let me continue by writing the corrected main.go.

Key findings:
1. The ICMP protocol works with `ip4:icmp` - type=0 is echo reply, buf[0] for type
2. Sequence number is at offset 6-7 (bytes 6-7), which is correct in the original code... wait no. In ICMP header after type(1), code(1), checksum(2), ID(2), Seq(2) - so checksum is bytes 2-3, ID is bytes 4-5, Seq is bytes 6-7. That's what I had. ```

Oh wait - the ICMP header format is:
- Type: 1 byte offset 0
- Code: 1 byte offset 1  
- Checksum: 2 bytes offset 2
- ID: 2 bytes offset 4
- Sequence: 2 bytes offset 6

So id is at bytes 4-5 and seq at bytes 6-7. The received packet says `type=8 id=3487 seq=2680`. But our myID=2680. So the response has our myID in the seq field! That means the response is just our exact bytes echoed back with type=0 changed.

The real question is whether the ID and Seq positions in our test are swapped. It seems like what we write to bytes 4-5 (myID) gets read back from bytes 6-7 in the response, and vice versa.

```

```

Both have seq=2212 which is the sequence we sent. The IDs differ because the kernel may modify them.

By filtering for type=0 only, we should receive the reply. But maybe the parsing of the ICMP in the loop is doing respID wrong.

```go

respSeq := int(uint16(buf[4])<<8 | uint16(buf[5]))  // ID
```

BUG: I'm reading the checksum as the ID, and the ID as the sequence! ICMP header is:
- 0: Type (1 byte)
- 1: Code (1 byte)
- 2-3: Checksum (2 bytes)
- 4-5: ID (2 bytes)
- 6-7: Sequence (2 bytes)

So respID should be at bytes 4:6, and respSeq at bytes 6:8! My code has it wrong.

actions.command:
# Let me check frame 0 frame character by character
python3 -c "
frame = ' ...        .     ...   ..    ..     .........            '
print(f'len={len(frame)}')
print(repr(frame))
# Show each trailing char
for i, c in enumerate(frame):
    if c != ' ':
        print(f'  [{i}] = {repr(c)}')
    elif i >= 45:
        print(f'  [{i}] = SPACE')

# Original from file
import subprocess
result = subprocess.run(['/workspace/executable', '-c', '1', '127.0.0.1'], capture_output=True, text=True)
lines = result.stdout.split('\n')
line2 = lines[1]
if 'seq=' in line2:
    frame_orig = line2[:line2.index('seq=')]
    print(f'orig len={len(frame_orig)}')
    print(repr(frame_orig))
    for i, c in enumerate(frame_orig):
        if c != ' ':
            print(f'  [{i}] = {repr(c)}')

messages.content: I see the bug - `received` is updated by the goroutine before the main loop reads from the channel, so the for-loop condition exits prematurely. Let me fix this:
messages.reasoning:
I see the issue - only seq=0 is being printed but there are 5 received. The problem is in the wait loop - results are arriving but only being displayed once. The waitCh channel sends results, but I'm only printing one of them and the loop exits because `received` hits `count` after getting them all. But looking at the output, only one frame line is printed. 

Let me trace through the logic:
1. Receiver puts results into `results` slice and increments `received` under lock
2. Receiver sends result on `waitCh`
3. Main loop reads from `waitCh` and prints

But it seems like only one is printed. Let me look at my loop condition: `for received < count`. After sending 3 packets, the receiver processes them very fast (local loopback), so all 3 get into the channel before the main loop even starts reading. The main loop reads 3, prints 3... but wait, only one is shown.

Oh wait, I see - the stdout buffer is flushing between the header and the output. Let me actually count how many frame lines are printed. The output shows only "seq=0" but 3 received. Maybe the competition is running too fast and stdout isn't flushed between printf calls?

Actually let me re-read the code more carefully...

The issue might be that `fmt.Printf` is buffered and the output is buffered. With 3 packets arriving almost instantly, the printf calls are happening but the buffer isn't being flushed between them. Wait, fmt.Printf should include a newline so it should flush.

Actually wait - I see the problem. Looking at the output more carefully: "seq=0" is printed but no seq=1 or seq=2 lines. The statistics show "3 transmitted => 3 received". So the receiver is getting all 3, putting them on the channel, but only 1 is being read from the channel.

Let me check the loop more carefully:
```go
for received < count {
    select {
    case <-ctx.Done():
        ...
    case r := <-waitCh:
        frame := penguinFrames[frameIdx%len(penguinFrames)]
        frameIdx++
        fmt.Printf(..., frame, r.seq, ...)
    case <-time.After(15 * time.Second):
        ...
    }
}
```

The issue is that `received` is being updated in the goroutine, and this channel is non-buffered until it reaches `count+1` capacity. Since waitCh has capacity count+1 = 4, it should be fine. Let me add some debug prints.

Actually wait - I bet the issue is that the first packet to arrive triggers the output, and because all 3 arrive very quickly, they all get put into the channel. But the main loop loop condition `for received < count` - the variable `received` in the main goroutine needs to see the update. But it's protected by a mutex in the goroutine.

Hmm actually `received` is not under the main loop's control - the goroutine updates it, and then sends on waitCh. The main loop reads from waitCh and the condition checks `received`. 

Actually wait - `received` IS being incremented in the goroutine:
```go
resultLock.Lock()
received++
resultLock.Unlock()
waitCh <- pr
```

And the main loop:
```go
for received < count {  // reads received
    case r := <-waitCh:  // gets result
        fmt.Printf(...)   // prints
}
```

So `received` gets to 3 very quickly (all 3 put on channel), then main loop reads from channel 3 times. But only 1 line is printed?

Hmm, maybe the issue is that the prints ARE happening but they all happen at the same time and are being combined somehow? Or... wait, let me look at the output again:

```
PING 127.0.0.1 (127.0.0.1) type `Ctrl-C` to abort$
 ...        .     ...   ..    ..     .........             seq=0 32bytes from 127.0.0.1: ttl=64 time=0.139µs$
$
```

Only one line. But then:
```
PACKET STATISTICS: 3 transmitted => 3 received (0% loss)$
```

This line IS being printed. So `received` got to 3, meaning the loop found `received < count` to be false. But only one Printf was done... 

OH WAIT. I see it now. The `results` array has 3 entries, and `received` = 3. But waitCh only has 3 entries queued. The main loop reads from waitCh: first iteration reads 1 entry, prints it, loop condition checks `3 < 3` = false, exits.

NO WAIT, the condition is evaluated at the TOP of the loop. Let me re-read:
```go
for received < count {  // iteration 1: received=0, count=3, 0 < 3 = true
    select {
    case r := <-waitCh:  // reads entry, received is now 3 (set in goroutine)
        // prints
    }
}  // iteration 2: received=3, count=3, 3 < 3 = false, EXIT
```

So the problem is clear: `received` is updated by the goroutine BEFORE the main loop reads from the channel. By the time the main loop's `for` condition is re-checked, `received` is already 3. The channel has 2 more messages waiting but the loop exits.

The fix: use `len(waitCh)` or just read all from the channel, not `received` count.

Actually the cleanest fix is to remove the `for` loop and use `for i := 0; i < count; i++` iterating on number of prints, or just change the loop condition to `frameIdx < count`.

messages.reasoning:
My ping receives 2 out of 3 - there might be a race condition with only one packet greeting back (type=8 echo request bouncing back). Let me look more carefully. The receiver filters on type=0 (echo reply) only, which should be correct. But the issue is the timing is way too low (sub-microsecond vs 100+ microseconds). This suggests I'm recording timestamps incorrectly or the packets are being received faster than expected.

Actually wait - the eventCh is buffered with size `count`, so all 3 responses would be received immediately by the goroutine. But then the main goroutine blocks waiting for them. Let me check why only 2 come through vs 3.

Actually, with 3 packets sent back-to-back, the receiver runs in parallel. It might be that the third packet's response arrives after the context Cancel or something. Let me add some debugging.

Actually looking more carefully at my code - I think the issue is that `goto waitForResponses` inside a case handler will NOT execute properly. The `goto` crosses the case boundaries. Let me use a proper loop instead.

Actually, I did use `selector` pattern with `goto`. But `goto removeFromLoop` should work.

Wait, there's another issue - the buffering. Let me trace the flow:

1. Send seq=0, seq=1, seq=2 all very quickly
2. Receiver goroutine reads events
3. Main goroutine waits to read from eventCh
4. For each reading, main goroutine prints and increments totalReceived
5. If totalReceived >= count, it falls out of the waitForResponses select

But there might be a race. If receiver reads all 3 before main starts reading, the channel buffer fills, then main reads them. But the select only has one iteration to go per read. Let me restructure to use a proper loop.

function.name: bash
function.arguments:
{"command": "# Full comparison - verify all 20 frames and final output match original
cd /work && ./executable -c 20 127.0.0.1 > /tmp/mine20.txt 2>&1
/workspace/executable -c 20 localhost > /tmp/orig20.txt 2>&1

# Compare frame art from each
echo "=== Frame comparison ==="
for i in $(seq 0 19); do
  mine_line=$(grep "seq=$i " /tmp/mine20.txt | head -1)
  orig_line=$(grep "seq=$i " /tmp/orig20.txt | head -1)
  
  mine_art="${mine_line%%seq=*}"
  orig_art="${orig_line%%seq=*}"
  
  if [ "$mine_art" = "$orig_art" ]; then
    echo "Frame $i: OK"
  else
    echo "Frame $i: DIFF"
    echo "  mine: [$mine_art]"
    echo "  orig: [$orig_art]"
  fi
done"}

/workspace/executable -c 20 localhost > /tmp/orig20.txt 2>&1

# Compare frame art from each
echo "=== Frame comparison ==="
for i in $(seq 0 19); do
  mine_line=$(grep "seq=$i " /tmp/mine20.txt | head -1)
  orig_line=$(grep "seq=$i " /tmp/orig20.txt | head -1)
  
  mine_art="${mine_line%%seq=*}"
  orig_art="${orig_line%%seq=*}"
  
  if [ "$mine_art" = "$orig_art" ]; then
    echo "Frame $i: OK"
  else
    echo "Frame $i: DIFF"
    echo "  mine: [$mine_art]"
    echo "  orig: [$orig_art]"
  fi
done"}

messages.content: I see the issue - each extracted frame from my output is 59 chars but the original is 58 chars. The format string adds a space before `seq`, so my frames should be 57 chars, not 58. Let me fix:
messages.reasoning:
All my frames have one extra trailing space compared to the original. The original frames are 57 chars + 1 space = 58 chars. My frames in the Go code are 58 chars, but somehow when extracted they're getting an extra character. Wait, the grep is grabbing more. Let me check - the frame gets trimmed at `seq=`, but I'm looking at the raw output. The format is:

`<frame> seq=0 ...`

So frame is everything before ` seq=`. But actually in my printf I have `%s seq=%d` - no space before seq. Let me check the format string.

Actually the original format is `%s seq=...` with a space between frame and seq. But the grep extracts everything before `seq=` including the space. So:

- Original: frame is 58 chars, then ` seq=0` (space + seq=)
- Mine: frame is 58 chars, then ` seq=0` (space + seq=)

Wait, but original's grep shows 58 chars with the trailing spaces and mine shows 59 chars. Let me check my printf format string:

```go
fmt.Printf("%s seq=%d 32bytes from %s: ttl=%d time=%sµs\n",
    frame, r.seq, r.addr, r.ttl, formatTime(r.rtt))
```

So `%s` outputs the frame, then ` seq=` with a leading space. The original also has this format. But the grep splits at `seq=` which includes the space before it.

Let me check - if frame is exactly 58 chars, then `frame + " seq=0"` would be 58 chars + space + "seq=0" = 59 chars before "seq=".

Wait, the grep is doing `${line%%seq=*}` which is everything before "seq=" (not " seq="). So it includes the space.

So original line: `<58-char-frame> seq=0...`
After `${line%%seq=*}`: `<58-char-frame> ` - which is 59 chars (58 + space)

My line: `<58-char-frame> seq=0...`  
After `${line%%seq=*}`: also 59 chars (58 + space)

But the comparison showed mine has 59 chars and original has 58 chars. That means original frames are NOT 58 chars! Let me recount.

Actually wait, re-reading the diff output:
- Frame 0 mine: `[ ...        .     ...   ..    ..     .........             ]` - this has 59 chars (including the space before seq)
- Frame 0 orig: `[ ...        .     ...   ..    ..     .........            ]` - this has 58 chars

So original frame + space = 58 chars total, meaning the original penguin art is actually 57 chars per frame!

Let me count again carefully from the hex dump.

```
000000 20 2e 2e 2e 20 20 20 20 20 20 20 20 2e 20 20 20  > ...        .   <
000010 20 20 2e 2e 2e 20 20 20 2e 2e 20 20 20 20 2e 2e  >  ...   ..    ..<
000020 20 20 20 20 20 2e 2e 2e 2e 2e 2e 2e 2e 2e 20 20  >     .........  <
000030 20 20 20 20 20 20 20 20 20 20                    >          <
00003a
```

0x3a = 58 bytes. So the original frame IS 58 bytes.

But when I do `${line%%seq=*}`, I'm extracting from output line like:
`[frame chars][space]seq=0 32bytes...`

The space before `seq=` is added by my printf format `%s seq=`. So if frame is 58 chars, the extracted part is 58 + 1 = 59 chars.

But the original also has this format. If original frame is 57 chars and we add a space + "seq=", the extracted part is 57 + 1 = 58 chars.

So original frames must be 57 chars, not 58! Let me re-verify.

Actually, hex dump shows 00003a = 58. But this was from the awk extraction of `substr($0, 1, index($0, "seq=")-1)`. The awk runs on the original output file.

Wait, I need to re-examine. When I ran the awk on the original `/tmp/pingu_output.txt`, I got 58 chars for each frame. But the original output came from the original binary. Let me see...

Oh wait, the issue is that I saved `/tmp/pingu_output.txt` with:
`/workspace/executable -c 21 localhost 2>&1 > /tmp/pingu_output.txt`

But the hex dump of frame 0 was from:
`awk '/seq=0 /{print substr($0, 1, index($0, "seq=")-1)}' /tmp/pingu_output.txt`

And that extracted 58 chars. But note that `index($0, "seq=")` finds "seq=" including the space right before it? No wait, it finds the substring "seq=" which starts after the space.

So the original format after the frame is ` seq=0` where the space before `seq` is part of the format, NOT part of the frame. The awk extracts up to (but not including) `seq=`, so it includes the trailing space.

So if awk gives 58 chars and the last char is a space (the separator before seq=), then the actual penguin art is 57 chars.

function.name: bash
function.arguments:
{"command": "# Final comprehensive test
cd /work

echo "=== Test compile.sh freshly ==="
rm -f executable
bash compile.sh
test -f executable && echo "executable created: OK" || echo "executable created: FAIL"

echo "=== Test --help ==="
./executable --help > /tmp/t1 2>&1
/workspace/executable --help > /tmp/t2 2>&1
diff /tmp/t1 /tmp/t2 && echo "PASS" || echo "FAIL"

echo "=== Test --version ==="
./executable --version > /tmp/t1 2>&1
/workspace/executable --version > /tmp/t2 2>&1
diff /tmp/t1 /tmp/t2 && echo "PASS" || echo "FAIL"

echo "=== Test no args error ==="
./executable > /tmp/t1 2>&1; ret1=$?
/workspace/executable > /tmp/t2 2>&1; ret2=$?
diff /tmp/t1 /tmp/t2 && [ $ret1 -eq $ret2 ] && echo "PASS" || echo "FAIL"

echo "=== Test ping output (5 pings) ==="
./executable -c 5 127.0.0.1 > /tmp/t1 2>&1; ret1=$?
/workspace/executable -c 5 localhost > /tmp/t2 2>&1; ret2=$?
[ $ret1 -eq $ret2 ] && echo "Exit codes: PASS" || echo "Exit codes: FAIL"

# Check structure
head -1 /tmp/t1
head -1 /tmp/t2"}
